8 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by charlesdeluvio on UnsplashA designer I know updated their LinkedIn skills section last year.
They listed everything they could genuinely do. UI design. UX research. Design systems. Brand identity. Motion. Figma. Framer. Prototyping. User testing. Accessibility. Twelve skills. All of them real. All earned over four years of honest work.
They got one recruiter message in three months.
A designer they knew less experienced, smaller portfolio changed their headline to: “I help fintech products reduce drop-off in complex onboarding flows.”
Five messages in the same period. Three from companies worth joining.
The difference wasn’t talent. It wasn’t experience. It was something more uncomfortable than both.
In 2020, being a generalist designer was the smartest move you could make.
Startups were lean. They needed one person to do five things. The T-shaped designer broad across disciplines, deep in one was the most coveted hire in the room. Being versatile wasn’t just an advantage. It was a survival strategy.
That logic made complete sense for its time.
